annotations.text: The symptoms of a hydatid cyst are caused principally by the pressure of the expanding cyst, which is estimated to grow between 1 and 5 cm a year.

The differential diagnosis of hepatic masses in children is broad. My task is to try to correlate the radiographic picture with the findings from the history, physical examination, and laboratory investigations.

Primary hepatic tumors are rare, but unfortunately nearly two thirds of them are malignant, representing the third most common abdominal cancer encountered in pediatric practice, after Wilms' tumor and neuroblastoma.

If the serologic test for amebae had been positive, further medical treatment would have been appropriate, since 72 hours are often required to observe a clinical response after the initiation of anti-amebic therapy.
Although I am troubled by the negative serologic test, there are certainly precedents for its occurrence. This diagnosis seems to fit the overall clinical picture best.

The radiographic features of this case are also suggestive of this diagnosis. The finding of a large, solitary mass within the right lobe of the liver is typical of an amebic abscess and is present in the great majority of the cases.
Fever, right-upper-quadrant abdominal pain, and malaise are the most prominent symptoms.
Amebiasis is estimated to occur in nearly 10 percent of the world's population and is certainly present in the Mediterranean area.
The history of Mediterranean travel forces me to consider strongly a parasitic cause of the hepatic mass.

The finding on the MRI scan of a sharply circumscribed, heterogeneous mass of low intensity on the T    –weighted images but of increased intensity on the T    –weighted images has been reported in MRI scans of known amebic liver absc
Caval compression by an amebic abscess has been described, even to the point of producing intraluminal thrombosis in the superior vena cava with extension into the right atrium.
This patient's acute presentation with fever, chills, an elevated white-cell count of 15,500, and a sedimentation rate of 81 mm per hour certainly suggests an infectious cause
One would have to invoke a superinfection to explain the clinical picture. That is clearly a possibility, but it is not a very attractive one.
Pyogenic liver abscesses must be included in the differential diagnosis. Such abscesses are very rare, particularly in otherwise normal children.
The ultrasound examination and the CT and MRI scans confirmed the presence of a large, solitary mass within the right lobe of the liver. No pulmonary or other intraabdominal disease was identified.

The problem with the diagnosis of a primary hepatic malignant tumor is the presentation. Tumors of this type typically present as abdominal masses, often with abdominal discomfort and weight loss; fever is rare, particularly the type of fever that this boy experienced.
The history of Mediterranean travel forces me to consider strongly a parasitic cause of the hepatic mass. Hydatid disease is common in the Mediterranean region.

The radiologic manifestations of an uncomplicated hepatic hydatid cyst are typical. It is largely filled with fluid, contains multiple daughter cysts, and usually has a hydatid sand layer in its base.
Benign hepatic lesions, such as choledochal cysts, cystadenomas, hamartomas, focal nodular hyperplasia, and congenital hepatic cysts, would be less likely to produce this radiographic appearance.

To make a simple hepatic hydatid cyst fit with the clinical scenario in this case, however, it would be necessary to invoke a secondary bacterial infection, which makes this diagnosis less attractive.

The major problem with this diagnosis is the negative enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) for anti-amebic antibodies on the fourth hospital day. This test is very sensitive and if properly performed should be positive in almost all cases.
The demonstration of a nonhomogeneous mass with septa by ultrasound and CT scans is nonspecific but certainly consistent with this diagnosis.
A vascular malformation or a highly vascular hepatic tumor is improbable in this case in view of the lack of enhancement on the studies with contrast material.

Therefore, sequential serologic testing and surgical intervention to obtain material for microscopical examination, Gram stain, and bacteriologic culture are warranted in patients with a hepatic abscess and nonreactive serologic tests for antibodies to  *Echin. histolytica*
Alternatively, there are cases of documented amebic hepatic abscesses with negative serologic testing.

The patient was seen by the Infectious Disease Service. We were impressed by his extensive travel history, and the history and findings on physical examination placed an amebic abscess at the top of our list of diagnoses.

We also considered echinococcal disease, although we thought that it was much less likely not only on an epidemiologic basis but also on the basis of the absence of a thick-walled capsule demonstrable in the various radiologic studies that were performed.

With the results of these studies we became very concerned by the predominantly solid nature of the mass, which would not be expected in amebic or echinococcal disease and made the possibility of a malignant tumor more likely.
The gross and histologic features of the case under discussion are typical, with round, spindle-shaped, and stellate cells in a myxoid background with admixed larger cells.
PAS-positive intracytoplasmic pink globules are present in the majority of the cases. The globules are thought to reflect a degenerative process.
The treatment for the embryonal sarcoma is surgical resection, with adjuvant chemotherapy and possibly radiation therapy.
The prognosis is generally poor, although there have been some reports of five-year survival.
Local regrowth is a problem, particularly in cases in which the tumor has been incompletely resected.
The metastases that have been reported were principally in the lung, bone, pleura, and peritoneum.

Immunohistochemical staining performed on formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue revealed tumor cells positive for vimentin but negative for alpha-fetoprotein, keratin, factor VIII, epithelial membrane antigen, and S-100 protein.
Staining for alpha    –antitrypsin showed diffuse positivity, particularly within the larger cells, although the pink globules did not sta

Rare cells showed positivity for desmin. Electron-microscopical examination revealed that the tumor-cell population was predominantly fibroblastic, with abundant dilated rough endoplasmic reticulum, although there were also scattered neoplastic histiocytes, with pseudopods, lysosomes, and lipid droplets.
Structures corresponding to the pink globules were not identified. These findings are consistent with those in previously reported studies.
The patient was referred to physicians in London, where he was placed on a "Stage 1" protocol for rhabdomyosarcoma, consisting of a six-month course of vincristine and actinomycin.
They chose not to give radiotherapy in view of the apparently complete resection of the tumor.

The patient is tolerating the chemotherapy well and is slowly increasing his daily activities, including returning to school, participating in a play, and taking long walks through the English countryside.
His physician in London has told us of his experience with two other patients treated with a similar protocol who are alive eight years and three months after the end of treatment.
